title: Spinning raw fleece on a drop spindle
subtitle: From unwashed lock to spun singles on a top-whorl drop spindle
excerpt: Learn to select, skirt, and spin directly from raw unwashed or minimally processed fleece on a top-whorl drop spindle. This beginner entry follows the process from handling a raw lock to producing a consistent singles without carding or combing.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional drop-spindle spinning practice. Spinning from raw or minimally processed lock is documented in British guild teaching; the lock-to-yarn process is a foundational beginner skill in handspinning.

glossaryTerms.definition: A naturally grouped cluster of wool fibres from a fleece, held together by crimp and a small amount of lanolin or vegetable matter. Each lock represents one growth period between shearings.
The process of removing the outer edges of a raw fleece before spinning: the legs, belly wool, and heavily soiled or felted sections that are not worth processing.
A drop-spindle technique in which the spindle is set spinning and then parked under the arm or on the leg while the spinner uses both hands to draft the fibre supply before releasing more twist.

content.text: and basic lock selection, but no carding or combing. The

method gives a beginner time to draft consistently without rushing to keep the spindle spinning. Raw or minimally washed fleece retains some lanolin that helps the fibres grip each other during drafting; thoroughly scoured fleece is a little harder to control at first.

attrs.title: Fleece selection
attrs.body: Choose a fleece with staple 8 to 12 cm and fine crimp. Avoid very coarse fleece for a first project.

items.name: Raw or lightly washed fleece, medium-fine crimp (Corriedale or BFL recommended)
Top-whorl drop spindle, medium weight (30 to 50 g)

content.text: Step 1: Skirt and select the fleece

Open the fleece cut side up. Remove the skirting (the outer 10 to 15 cm around the edge) and discard any sections that are felted, heavily soiled, or full of vegetable matter. Select locks from the shoulder area of the fleece, which has the most consistent staple length and the finest fibre.

Hold a lock by the cut end and gently tug the tip end to open the lock structure slightly. Remove any large pieces of vegetable matter by picking them out. If the lock is heavily lanoliny, a brief rinse in warm (not hot) water and a gentle squeeze dry will make it easier to draft.

Attach a leader thread to the spindle. Set the spindle spinning clockwise with a sharp pinch between thumb and finger. Park the spindle against your leg or under your arm. With both hands, draft a short length of lock (3 to 5 cm) by holding the lock near the leader with one hand and drawing the fibres out gently with the other. Release the spindle, let the twist run up into the drafted fibre, then park again and draft the next section.

When the spun length reaches the spindle cup, wind the yarn onto the shaft. Leave a short leader above the cup. Continue spinning and drafting through the rest of the lock supply. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy, wash the skein in warm water to remove remaining lanolin, and hang to dry.

items.problem: The spindle drops repeatedly before the yarn is wound on
items.cause: The yarn is too short to support the spindle weight, or the twist was not set firmly enough
items.fix: Wind onto the shaft more frequently, leaving a longer leader. Use a lighter spindle for the first few sessions.

items.problem: The drafted section drifts apart before twist enters
items.cause: Drafting too long a section in one pull, or the lock was not opened sufficiently
items.fix: Draft shorter sections (2 to 3 cm at a time) and keep the pinch hand close to the twist entry point.
